# China's BYD opens EV factory in Thailand, first in Southeast Asia

[WATCH LIVE](/live-tv/)

China's BYD opens its first electric vehicle factory in Southeast Asia, a fast-growing regional EV market, in Rayong, Thailand, on July 4, 2024.

Chalinee Thirasupa | Reuters

China's [BYD](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/002594.SZ) opened an electric vehicle plant in Thailand on Thursday, the automaker's first factory in Southeast Asia, a fast-growing regional EV market where it has become the [dominant player](https://reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/chinese-automakers-retain-grip-over-southeast-asias-booming-electric-car-market-2024-06-21/).

"Thailand has a clear EV vision and is entering a new era of auto manufacturing," BYD CEO and President Wang Chuanfu said at the opening ceremony. "We will bring technology from China to Thailand."

The BYD plant is part of a [wave of investment](https://www.reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/china-led-ev-boom-thailand-threatens-japans-grip-key-market-2023-07-09/) worth more than $1.44 billion from Chinese EV makers who are setting up factories in Thailand, helped by government subsidies and tax incentives.

Hong Kong-listed shares of BYD , the world's largest EV maker, climbed 1.6% to HK$235, after hitting their highest levels in a week.

Thailand is a regional auto assembly and export hub, and has long been dominated by Japanese car makers such as [Toyota Motor,](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/7203.T) [Honda Motor Co](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/7267.T) and [Isuzu Motors](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/7202.T).

By 2030, the country aims to convert 30% of its annual production of 2.5 million vehicles into EVs, according to a government plan.

"BYD is using Thailand as a production hub for export to ASEAN and many other countries," said Narit Therdsteerasukdi, secretary-general of Thailand's Board of Investment, referring to the 10-nation Southeast Asian bloc.

As part of its expansion outside China, BYD is [building its first](https://www.reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/chinese-ev-makers-plans-make-cars-europe-2024-05-07/) European production base in Hungary.

Set to launch operations in three years, the BYD facility will produce EVs and plug-in hybrids for the European market, where the European Commission is [imposing tariffs](https://www.reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/china-based-ev-makers-hit-with-european-union-tariffs-2024-06-12/) of up to nearly 38% on Chinese-made EVs.

BYD's China-made EVs will incur tariffs of about 17%.

The sprawling Thai facility, [announced two years ago](https://www.reuters.com/business/autos-transportation/chinas-byd-set-up-ev-plant-thailand-2022-09-08/) and worth $490 million, will have a production capacity of 150,000 vehicles per year, including plug-in hybrids.

The right-hand-drive EVs manufactured at the plant will potentially allow BYD to circumvent EU tariffs, which are applicable to China-made vehicles.

"We will also assemble batteries and other important parts here," said Liu Xueliang, BYD's Asia Pacific general manager.

Thailand is the largest overseas market for BYD, which commanded a 46% share of country's EV segment in the first quarter and is the third-largest player in passenger cars, according to research firm Counterpoint.

Other EV rivals in the local market include [Great Wall Motor](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/601633.SS), which also has a production facility in Thailand, and U.S. automaker [Tesla](https://www.reuters.com/markets/companies/TSLA.O).
